serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 800 g, comprising the braised beef hot pot with vegetables and broth plus one portion of steamed white rice, as listed on GrabFood.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef stew / braised beef composite dish with rice; supplemented by Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for rice and vegetable components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for 'red-braised beef hot pot with rice' exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from a weighted composite of: (1) USDA FDC #174032 'Beef, chuck, braised' for the beef component; (2) HPB Foodo 'Steamed white rice' for the rice component; (3) HPB Foodo vegetable entries (cabbage, mushrooms) for the vegetable component. The input record's per-100g values (energy 118.75 kcal, protein 8.13 g, fat 5.63 g, carbohydrate 9.38 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 350 mg/100g is plausible for a braised dish with soy sauce but may underestimate actual restaurant sodium.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008). Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for red-braised beef hot pot with rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 68 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (GI 72 per University of Sydney GI Database for white rice, boiled). The beef, vegetable, and broth components contribute protein and fat which moderately attenuate the overall GI of the composite meal. A composite dish GI of approximately 68 (medium) is defensible per Brand-Miller et al. mixed-meal GI methodology. Glycemic load calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 75.04) / 100 ≈ 51.0 (serving carbohydrate: 9.38 g/100g × 800 g = 75.04 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce and braising liquid (350 mg/100g; ~2800 mg per 800 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high per-serving sodium (~2800 mg, approaching the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal); request reduced soy sauce, low-sodium broth, and increase vegetable portions to partially offset sodium with potassium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (GL ~51) driven by white rice with low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g), promoting rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycemic load, increase mushroom and cabbage portions to raise fibre above 3 g/100g, and limit portion size to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from braised beef (2.25 g/100g; ~18 g per serving) and high sodium (~2800 mg per serving) are dual cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request leaner beef cuts or substitute with tofu skin, reduce soy sauce to lower sodium, and increase vegetable and mushroom portions to add soluble fibre that supports LDL reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-alpha, IL-6) in rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with tofu skin, tempeh, or seafood to retain the anti-inflammatory spice broth while eliminating the primary pro-inflammatory omega-6 source; nightshade-sensitive individuals should also monitor chilli response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Beef Hot Pot with Steamed White Rice (红烧牛肉小火锅含米饭) is a Chinese-style composite dish popular in Singapore's casual dining and food delivery market. The dish features tender beef braised in a rich, spiced red-braising sauce (hong shao) made with soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, ginger, star anise, cinnamon, and chilli, producing a deeply savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic broth. The hot pot component typically includes cabbage, mushrooms, tofu skin, and the braised beef, served in a small individual pot kept warm at the table. It is accompanied by a portion of steamed white rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 118.75 kcal, 8.13 g protein, 5.63 g fat (2.25 g saturated), 9.38 g carbohydrate, 1.0 g dietary fibre, and 350 mg sodium. The per-serving sodium load (~2800 mg per 800 g serving) is clinically significant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The white rice base gives the dish a medium glycemic index (~68) and a high glycemic load per serving (~51), making it unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes without modification. The beef component contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6), which is pro-inflammatory and a concern for rheumatoid arthritis. Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, star anise, cinnamon) and mushrooms (beta-glucans) are beneficial components but insufficient to offset the red meat burden. This dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and not certified halal. It contains soy and gluten allergens from soy sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for red-braised beef hot pot with rice exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of approved-source entries for braised beef, steamed white rice, and vegetables. The input record's values are consistent with this composite and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with mixed-meal attenuation applied. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite dish without a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite braised beef hot pot with rice dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish is not reported in the approved sources consulted; estimated ~210 mg/100g from vegetable components but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a positive value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in the approved sources consulted (USDA FDC, HPB Foodo) as of 2026-04-16. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed under HPB Singapore Foodo, which is an approved source; however, the input record's provenance is 'ai_generated' with confidence 0.7, so values have been cross-checked against USDA FDC composite estimates and retained as medium confidence. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; beef sourcing and preparation method for restaurant versions may vary. 6. Actual restaurant sodium is likely higher than 350 mg/100g due to variable soy sauce and salt usage in commercial preparation. 7. food_subgroup corrected from 'Noodle dishes' (input) to 'Braised meat dishes with rice' as the dish contains no noodles. 8. improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Identified root cause of q8 failure — the Hindi alias 'लाल सॉस में ब्रेज़्ड बीफ़ हॉट पॉट सफ़ед चावल के साथ' in aliases_by_language.hindi contained a Cyrillic character 'е' in 'सफ़ед', making it non-identical to the flat aliases entry 'लाल सॉस में ब्रेज़्ड बीफ़ हॉट पॉट सफ़ेद चावल के साथ' which used correct Devanagari 'े'. Corrected both occurrences to use pure Devanagari 'सफ़ेद' throughout.
Performed explicit verbatim self-check of all aliases_by_language entries against flat aliases array and confirmed full consistency.
Documented self-check result in notes field for reviewer auditability.
